stories.title: The Salt-Thief's Gambit
stories.text: Marin the Copper-Handed had stolen from every merchant in Saltmere's quarter—everyone but one. The Spice-Lord Corvin kept his vault seven fathoms below the docks, guarded by tidal-magic and a contract written in merfolk blood. Three months since the Remaking, gold meant nothing. But spice—cinnamon, starflower, saffron—those still moved in the shadow-markets. Those still fed people. Marin approached the harbormaster's tower at dusk, her copper fingers humming a locksmith's old song. The harbor itself was wrong now: water that remembered its currents, ships half-submerged in decades-old positions. The Remaking had shattered Saltmere's trade routes. Refugees crowded the docks. And Corvin was raising prices on the last legitimate supply. She descended the stone stairs wet with spray. The vault-lock was bone and silver—merfolk-work, singing faintly. Marin pressed her copper palm against it and felt the lock recognize something in her touch: a thief's certainty, a hand that had opened a thousand doors. The vault breathed open. Inside, spice in earthenware vessels. And below them, something else—a ledger written in blood-ink, its pages moving as if alive.

stories.title: The Hawk-Keeper's Pact
stories.text: Ashara the Archer had kept to the high moors for forty winters, and Ashira—her hawk—had been with her for thirty of them. The bird was old now, her wing-beats labored, but her eyes were still sharp as spear-points. They poured north from the Burned Expanse, gaunt and fractured, some carrying phantom-limbs where family members had been scattered across timelines. Ashara had lived alone by choice. She didn't want their pleas. But Ashira landed on her shoulder one dawn and flew due north, insistent as prophecy. Ashara followed. They found the wolf-spirit in a ravine—old, bleeding, pinned beneath a killing-stone that someone had left in the old days of wardcraft. The spirit was neither wholly beast nor wholly fae, and it was *dying*. It had been guardian of these passes. The Remaking had shattered the covenant it kept; the stone that bound it had come loose. Ashira landed on the spirit's brow and sang—not hawk-speech, but something older. The killing-stone cracked. Ashara pulled, using strength forty winters of solitude had earned her. The spirit rose.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Daughter
stories.text:
Messa was fourteen when her mother died in the kilns—not burned, but *transformed*, her body becoming translucent glass that sang when the light struck it. The furnace-houses of Keshtir had always demanded payment. The Remaking just changed what currency meant. Now consciousness-fragments were worth more than blood. And Messa, alone in the Lampwick Quarter, understood something the auditors didn't: glass *remembered*. Every piece her grandmother had forged carried memory in its lattice. The kilns themselves were archives. Her mother's fragment had dissolved into the furnace—she'd watched it happen, unable to stop it. "Then you work for us," the lead auditor said, his ledger already rewriting itself to claim her labor. "Or your workshop burns."

Messa smiled—not sweetly, but with the certainty of someone who'd spent fourteen years reading fire. She didn't refuse. She agreed. And then she began to forge. Every piece of glass she made was a phantom-ledger in miniature—contradictory, impossible, singing of three different truths at once. She wove them into necklaces, bowls, mirror-shards that showed the viewer not their reflection but their doubt.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Mirror
stories.text: Captain Mereth had drowned once already—the day the Tear of the Drowned God began to fail. That failure was reflected in her own soul. She surfaced three different captains: Mereth-Echo, Mereth-Mirror, and the original, and they could not agree on which covenant to offer the dying god. The original Mereth wanted blood-sacrifice, the old way. Echo-Mereth proposed a tidal bargain—the deepkin would surrender a third of their pearl-wealth to renew the seal. Mirror-Mereth wanted to *let it break*, to reshape the boundary entirely and call up new treaties with the surface kingdoms. They were drowning each other's thoughts. Venture to the Reef-Stone, the Keeper of Pearls had commanded. Confront your fracture. Choose one voice, or the abyss chooses for you. Mereth—all three of them—descended to where the Tear hung like a dying star in the deep water. It was failing faster now, cracks spider-webbing across its crystalline surface. The pre-human dead that guarded it were singing in desperation. She saw, then, what her fracture had hidden: the original Mereth had always been terrified.

stories.title: The Merchant's Resurrection
stories.text: Lysander was half-luminous before the Remaking, a threshold-being caught between mortal flesh and something else. After it, he was mostly light—walking geometry of starlight pressed into almost-human shape, perpetually incomplete. He arrived at the Wailing Scar market when it shimmered between three different versions of itself—Hearthlands autumn, Ashen Reach winter, and a season that hadn't existed before the cosmic working. The Scar was the one place where fractured realities overlapped, where phantom-ledgers of three different worlds could be traded for consciousness-fragments or spice or secrets. Lysander didn't need to eat. But he needed to *speak*, and the Scar was full of voices. He found them by the singing-stones: seventeen transformed merchants, suspended at the threshold between transcendence and dissolution. They were the ones the bone-singers had tried to teach, the ones who might become something entirely new. They were also dying, caught in a recursive moment where progress was impossible. "I remember," Lysander said, and his light dimmed to show them his own fracture. "I remember being whole. I remember the old stars.

stories.title: The Spice-Merchant's Daughter
stories.text:
Kestra had inherited her father's ledger-house in Pelimor's Merchant Quarter on 2026-06-21, the day the stars rewrote themselves. By sunset, his carefully tallied fortunes—measured in gold and coin—had become ash. By dawn, consciousness-fragments became the new weight in the vault. She refused to trade in souls. Instead, Kestra reopened the spice routes her father had abandoned years before, routes that cut through the Ashen Reach where the old songs still sang. She turned them away with tea and courtesy. "My business traffics in flavor," she told them. "Not in what fragments a soul."

On the third week, a caravan master named Torvin arrived dust-worn and desperate. His crew had fractured when the Remaking hit; some took work for the wyvern-lords, hunting consciousness-shards. He sought spices to trade with the deepkin, hoping to broker a path across the boundary-waters to find his daughter, scattered into a parallel iteration. Kestra gave him cardamom and saffron from her remaining stock—pre-Remaking spices, precious now. "The Deepkin Keeper of Pearls trades in oaths and memory," she said. "Not fragments.

stories.title: The Bone-Thief's Vigil
stories.text: Corvhen had walked three days without sleep, the consciousness-fragment burning against her sternum like a coal. The Dominion auditors were close—she could hear their ledger-songs scraping the air behind her, trying to rewrite the theft before it happened. She ducked into the waystation at Pale Crossing, where refugees still gathered despite the shortages, and found Naela the bone-singer waiting as if she'd always known Corvhen would arrive. "You stole the wrong fragment," Naela said, not unkindly. "That one remembers who it was before the Remaking. The auditors will burn the world to recover it." Corvhen's fingers found the bone-carved reliquary at her neck. Inside, the fragment sang in a voice she almost recognized—her sister's, maybe, or her own from before. "Then we teach it to forget," Corvhen whispered. "Before they arrive." Naela smiled, and began to sing in the Elder Tongue.

stories.title: The Gambler's Debt
stories.text:
The Shadow-Market beneath Anthrim had moved three times since the Remaking, always staying one ledger-sweep ahead of the Dominion's auditors. Marin the Copper-Handed had run it from the Lampwick Quarter's furnace-cellars, where heat and old songs made ledger-magic run sluggish. But Marin owed a debt she could not trade away: a consciousness-fragment borrowed from a demon's broker five years past, sworn to be repaid in kind or blood. She was a woman with no face—her features slipped like water, impossible to hold. "The Remaking has made your debt valuable," the faceless woman said. "One consciousness-fragment, or I take three of yours."

Marin had stolen six fragments from the Dominion's vaults in the week following the Ascension. She'd been planning to scatter them to refugee scribes for safekeeping. Now she had to choose: let refugees scatter into endless iteration, or surrender herself to fragmentation—her mind split across three vessels, conscious in pieces. She thought of Gareth, the old locksmith she'd saved from execution.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Choice
stories.text:
Echo-Kalith had been a reflection once—a fractured piece of the Deepkin Captain Kalith, split when the Tear of the Drowned God began to fail. Now she was herself, autonomous and real, standing at the boundary-platform where surface and abyss met. Above, the surface kingdoms were negotiating frantically: five-part sacrifice, tidal covenant, blood-oath renewal. The deepkin council wanted certainty. They wanted death, if necessary, to seal what was breaking. Echo-Kalith wanted something else. She called the merfolk-touched merchants—the Seventeen who'd been suspended at transcendence since the Remaking. They arrived at the boundary-waters, neither fully human nor fully transformed, singing in harmonics that made the seal vibrate. "The Tear was meant to hold two worlds apart," Echo-Kalith said. "But it was built on sacrifice—on the Drowned God's loneliness, on separation as currency. What if we offered it something else?"

She led them to the Sapphire Deep's heart, where the original seal lay: a pearl the size of a fist, pulsing with the light of drowned stars.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Gift
stories.text:
Henryk had gone blind to stop the ninth map from being drawn—a sacrifice that should have prevented the Tenth Ascension. But three weeks after the Remaking, maps were drawing themselves. The Counter-Archive had found eight previous iterations charted in stone, in bone, in the memory of refugees who'd lived through cycles the world had forgotten. Henryk was teaching refugees to navigate by memory instead. In a waystation library north of Pelimor, a young cartographer's apprentice named Aldrin sat at a table covered in blank parchment. "How do I map what I cannot see?" she asked. Henryk smiled—a thing he hadn't done since his eyes were lost. "The same way I navigate now. You describe the journey, not the lines. You listen to water, wind, the way a forest changes. You let other travelers add their steps to yours."

Aldrin began to write.
